title: Echinacea root tincture for immune support
subtitle: A concentrated tincture taken at the first sign of a cold
excerpt: Echinacea purpurea root macerated in 40% vodka for four to six weeks. Taken in short courses at the first sign of a cold or infection. The most extensively studied traditional immune herb in western herbal medicine, with multiple systematic reviews supporting short-course use.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve, A Modern Herbal (1931), echinacea entry, introduced to British herbalism from the American eclectic tradition. King's American Dispensatory (Felter and Lloyd, 1898), echinacea as a blood purifier and infection-fighter. WHO Monograph on Echinacea purpurea (1999), immune stimulation, clinical evidence overview. EMA HMPC monograph on Echinaceae purpureae radix (2015), confirms traditional use for prevention and treatment of common cold symptoms.

recipe.batchNotes: Double or triple the batch. Shelf life is 2 years in a sealed amber bottle.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Allow 4, 6 weeks of maceration before the tincture is ready. Make a batch in advance of cold season rather than waiting until you are already ill.

recipeTools.notes: A 500 ml glass jar with a tight lid.
For pressing and straining the finished tincture.
30 ml amber dropper bottle for the finished tincture.

glossaryTerms.definition: A preparation that modifies the activity of the immune system. In the context of echinacea, this refers to short-term stimulation of non-specific immune defence responses.

content.text: Echinacea root packed into a jar with vodka, left to macerate for four to six weeks, then strained and bottled for short-course use at the first sign of infection. Echinacea is the most extensively studied traditional

in western herbalism, dozens of clinical trials and multiple systematic reviews support its use for reducing the duration and severity of the common cold when taken early. The EMA's 2015 monograph confirms traditional use for both prevention (during exposure) and treatment of early cold symptoms. The tincture is the most effective preparation: it concentrates the alkamides that are primarily responsible for the immune-stimulating action and delivers them in a bioavailable form.

attrs.title: Important safety notes
attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a qualified herbalist or doctor for ongoing or serious symptoms.

Asteraceae allergy: echinacea is in the Asteraceae family. Anyone with a known allergy to ragweed, chrysanthemum, chamomile, or calendula is at higher risk of allergic reaction to echinacea preparations, use with caution and patch-test before internal use.

Immunosuppressants: echinacea is an immune stimulant. Do not use alongside immunosuppressant medications (used after organ transplant, for autoimmune conditions, or for certain cancers) without medical advice, the combination is theoretically antagonistic.

Autoimmune conditions: traditional herbalism has debated the use of immune stimulants in autoimmune conditions. Current clinical guidance is to seek specialist advice before using echinacea if you have rheumatoid arthritis, lupus, multiple sclerosis, or similar conditions.

Course length: echinacea is traditionally used in short courses, 7 to 10 days maximum. Continuous long-term use is not recommended by the EMA and may paradoxically reduce immune stimulation over time. Take a break of at least two weeks between courses.

Pregnancy: data on echinacea in pregnancy is limited. One prospective cohort study found no increased risk at usual doses in the first trimester, but the traditional guidance is to seek herbalist or midwife advice before using echinacea during pregnancy.

Alcohol content: each 2 ml dose contains approximately 0.8 ml of vodka (roughly 0.7 units).

items.prepNote: dried Echinacea purpurea root, cut and sifted

content.text: Place {{dried-echinacea-root}} of dried echinacea root in the maceration jar. Pour {{vodka-40-percent}} of vodka over. The spirit should cover the root completely, with at least 1 cm above the top of the herb. Seal tightly and label with the date. Shake the jar daily for the first week.
Macerate 4, 6 weeks, then press and strain

After 4 to 6 weeks in a cool, dark place, strain the contents through muslin-lined sieve into a jug. Gather the muslin and press firmly to extract all remaining liquid. The finished tincture will be dark amber-brown with a slightly peppery, aromatic taste, echinacea root has a distinctive tingling sensation on the tongue, which is normal and indicates the alkamide content. Decant into amber dropper bottles.

Adults: 2, 4 ml (40, 80 drops) taken in a small amount of water, up to three times daily, for a maximum of 7 to 10 days per course. Start at the first sign of a cold or infection. Take a break of at least two weeks between courses. Do not take continuously. For prevention during exposure to infection, 2 ml twice daily for up to ten days is within the traditional range.
Finished tincture keeps 2 years in a sealed amber bottle at room temperature away from light and heat.
When NOT to use this remedy

Do not use with immunosuppressant medication. Discuss with prescriber if you have an autoimmune condition. Avoid if you have an Asteraceae allergy. Do not use for more than 10 consecutive days, always take a break between courses. See your doctor for infections that are worsening rather than improving, for fever above 38.5°C that persists beyond 48 hours, or for any infection in an immunocompromised person.
